title: HW1 Q6 — Guess $T(n) = n^p + c$ for $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + 1$
statement: $T(1) = 0$, $T(2) = 1$, and for $n > 2$: $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + 1$. Try $T(n) = \sqrt{n}$, $n$, $n^2$; which one almost satisfies the recurrence? Then set $T(n) = n^p + c$, pick $p$ and solve for $c$.

steps.title: Try $T(n) = \sqrt{n}$.
steps.body:
LHS $= \sqrt{n}$. RHS $= \sqrt{n/3} + \sqrt{2n/3} + 1 = \sqrt{n}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} + \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}\right) + 1 = \sqrt{n} \cdot \frac{1 + \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} + 1 \approx 1.39\sqrt{n} + 1$.

RHS $>$ LHS by a multiplicative factor. The recurrence over-produces — $\sqrt{n}$ is too small.

steps.title: Try $T(n) = n$.
steps.body:
LHS $= n$. RHS $= n/3 + 2n/3 + 1 = n + 1$.

LHS $<$ RHS, but **only by the additive 1**. They are essentially equal. This is the right shape — the 'almost satisfies' case. The fix should involve absorbing that extra $+1$.

steps.title: Try $T(n) = n^2$.
steps.body:
LHS $= n^2$. RHS $= (n/3)^2 + (2n/3)^2 + 1 = n^2/9 + 4n^2/9 + 1 = 5n^2/9 + 1$.

LHS $>$ RHS by a multiplicative factor. $n^2$ is too big.

steps.title: Set $T(n) = n + c$ and solve for $c$.
steps.body:
From the linear case: LHS $=$ RHS nearly, off by the $+1$. Try $T(n) = n + c$:

LHS $= n + c$. RHS $= (n/3 + c) + (2n/3 + c) + 1 = n + 2c + 1$.

Equal means $n + c = n + 2c + 1$, i.e. $c = -1$.

So $T(n) = n - 1$ exactly satisfies the recurrence.

**Verification:** $T(n) = n - 1$. RHS $= (n/3 - 1) + (2n/3 - 1) + 1 = n - 1$. ✓ And base cases: $T(1) = 0 = 1 - 1$. ✓ $T(2) = 1 = 2 - 1$. ✓

tips.label: Why the shape $n^p$
tips.content: The sum $1/3 + 2/3 = 1$ puts this in the 'balanced' case where $\Theta(n \log n)$ usually wins. But the additive term is $1$ not $n$, so the balance shifts: linear already overshoots the additive work, so the exact solution is linear.

answerKey: $T(n) = n - 1$ satisfies the recurrence exactly. So $T(n) = \Theta(n)$.
